---
title: "Report"
authors:
  - name: "Oscar Plaisant"
    note: "22500288"
    url: "https://osk42.quarto.pub"
    email: "oscar.plaisant@etu.u-paris.fr"
  - name: "Anthony Saab"
  - name: "Yousra Tersi"

date: 2026-03-07

execute: 
  enabled: false
  eval: true
  echo: false
  cache: false

toc: true
notebook-links: global # global: show with toc, inline: show with code

notebook-view:
  - notebook: report.ipynb
    title: "report code title"

#format:
#  html:
#    output-file: report.html


# abstract: petit résumé
---



# Introduction and motivation

This study has utilized two independent data sets: The first represents national productivity through GDP per Hour Worked (GDPHRS) in U.S. Dollars using Purchasing Power Parity (PPP). The second measures the gender wage gap through the difference between the median wages earned by males and females, expressed as a percentage of the median earnings of males. These are important indicators of societal inequities and are significant because they represent large-scale social issues.
 In selecting the GDP per Hour Worked (GDPHRS), we were motivated to select a precision measure of labor efficiency. In contrast to GDP per capita (which divides the total amount of goods produced and services provided by the entire population) GDP per hour worked measures the exact amount of goods and services produced per hour of work. We chose to use USD Purchasing Power Parity (PPP) to account for differences in the cost of living and inflation rates so that a given unit of output produced in Colombia can be compared to a similar unit produced in Luxembourg in terms of its ability to purchase goods and services.
The gender wage gap is defined as the difference between the median wage earned by males and females divided by the median wage earned by males and then multiplied by 100. This is a key macro indicator of inequality; however, this metric is unadjusted and does not consider the potential differences in occupations, education, or hours worked between genders. Therefore, it primarily addresses structural and systemic differences in earning potential based upon gender within the larger economy. A positive number means males earn more than females, while a negative number means females earn more than males.
To answer a very important question regarding the relationship between gender wage disparity and national productivity, we plan to graph the gender wage gap against GDP per Hour Worked. Through this graphical analysis, we hope to determine if there is a correlation between high national economic productivity and reduced gender wage disparities. Additionally, we will examine whether highly productive advanced economies automatically reduce gender-based earning disparities or if the gender wage gap exists regardless of the wealth-generating capability of the economy.


# Results

{{< embed report.ipynb#tables >}}

A comparison of the time-series animated mapping of the Gender Wage Gap vs. GDP per Hour Worked to the scatter plot (from 2022) illustrates many patterns that call into question an assumption of economic development resolving inequality.
One of the first observations that comes out of looking at the data is the significant heterogeneity present in the data -- there is no direct linear correlation in how economically productive each country is and how equitable they distribute income between genders. For example, although South Korea (KOR) has an extremely high level of productivity ($53 USD PPP/hour), it has the largest gap in the data set at over 31%, while the large productivity European countries, Belgium (BEL) have gaps that range just under 1%. Also, of interest is the case of Luxembourg (LUX) with the highest productivity level in the data ($126 USD PPP/hour), and the lowest (negative) gap (-8.6%) showing that the average wage of women is higher than the average wage of men.
These findings raise important questions about the data. Why do countries with lower levels of productivity, i.e., Colombia (COL), Costa Rica (CRI), show very small gaps (less than 2%)? Is the wage gap reflecting a true difference in the treatment of women and men in the work place or is it simply that neither gender has access to high paying jobs thereby making the wage distribution in those countries so flat that everyone makes the same amount of money regardless of gender? Furthermore, the large productivity of countries like Japan (JPN) and Israel (ISR), which show a wage gap greater than 20% indicate that there may be some deep-rooted cultural beliefs, occupational separation, or "motherhood penalty" that creates barriers to equal opportunity for women in their respective labor markets.
Further analysis of the data from 2020-2023 using the animation shows a great deal of stability (stationarity). The data points on the scatter plots do not dramatically move up/down/left/right from year to year. Therefore, both the nation's productivity and structural wage gaps are "macroeconomic sticky" features. They don't respond well to quick changes, and they will only respond over time to large structural changes.
The most important thing to remember when using this data to make conclusions is to avoid making causal statements about the relationship shown in the scatter plot. The scatter plot doesn't indicate that an increase in a country's Gross Domestic Product (GDP) per hour worked causes the gender wage gap to decrease or grow. It shows that a country's ability to produce goods and services, and how equally men and women are treated within a country's borders, are two separate things that are interrelated through many factors, including but not limited to: the country's policies, childcare availability, and social norms/history. The data,  and only the data, suggest that generating wealth is not sufficient to ensure equal pay for work.


# Appendix

## Setting up the environment
In order to run this project, you have to initialize a virtual environment and to install required packages. It can be done this way :

```{sh}
python3 -m venv env
source env/bin/activate
pip3 install -r requirements.txt
```


## The code
You can reproduce graphics from this report using this code

```python
{{< include report.py >}}
```
